perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign nondisclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence for the benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall restrict access to Confidential Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is required.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'strictest,' 'sole and exclusive,' 'carefully,' 'reasonably' and the elimination of the requirement for third parties to sign non-disclosure agreements weakens the protections for confidential information. This could create issues under trade secret laws which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the qualifiers 'strictest,' 'sole and exclusive,' 'carefully,' and 'reasonably,' along with removing the requirement for signed nondisclosure agreements from relevant parties, significantly reduces the required effort and potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable under the circumstances' standard, thereby weakening trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The original contract includes terms that ensure stricter protection of confidential information, such as 'strictest,' 'sole and exclusive,' 'carefully,' and 'reasonably,' along with a requirement for third parties to sign non-disclosure agreements. The changed text removes these qualifiers and the requirement for non-disclosure agreements, thereby weakening the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this reduction in effort potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable under the circumstances' standard required by the UTSA for trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation indicate that the modifications significantly reduce the effort to maintain secrecy, creating a plausible legal contradiction under trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party records pertaining to Confidential Information if Disclosing Party requests it.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'any and all', 'notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials', 'in its possession' and 'immediately' and 'in writing', the clause is significantly weakened. A verbal request is not documented. Not returning 'all' materials can cause major issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California law, misappropriation can include continued possession or use of trade secrets after the termination of the agreement. By omitting the requirement to return 'any and all' confidential information immediately, the agreement increases the risk of misappropriation liability under CUTSA, as the Receiving Party may retain some materials, potentially leading to continued use or disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation. The original text requires the Receiving Party to return 'any and all' records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing. The modified text only requires the Receiving Party to return records pertaining to Confidential Information if Disclosing Party requests it. By removing the requirement to return 'any and all', 'notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials', 'in its possession' and 'immediately' and 'in writing', the Receiving Party could argue they don't need to return all confidential information. California Code, CIV 3426.2 (a) states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' The weaker return obligation in the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation by allowing the Receiving Party to retain some confidential materials.

perturbation.original_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement. Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall end at termination of the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that the confidentiality duty ends at termination, the contract fails to protect trade secrets for the period after the agreement ends. This directly contradicts laws that provide ongoing protection for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Limiting the NDA's effect to the agreement's term allows misappropriation immediately following termination, undermining the protection afforded by the EEA. Trade secrets can continue to be trade secrets beyond the life of a contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets after the termination of the agreement. The original text ensures confidentiality continues until the information is no longer a trade secret or the Disclosing Party releases the Receiving Party. The changed text, however, stipulates that the confidentiality duty ends at the agreement's termination, potentially allowing misappropriation of trade secrets immediately following termination. The Law Explanation provided, referencing the Economic Espionage Act, states that the altered NDA's effect undermines the protection afforded by the EEA. The scraped snippet names the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. While the snippet itself provides no specific definitions, the Law Explanation bolsters the determination that the modified contract language would contravene the aim of protecting trade secrets after the termination of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: If a court finds any provision of this Agreement invalid or unenforceable, the remainder of this Agreement shall be interpreted so as best to effect the intent of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: If a court finds any provision of this Agreement invalid or unenforceable, the remainder of this Agreement will be considered void.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the severability clause's attempt to preserve the contract's intent, and states the contract is entirely void should a provision be found invalid. This is not standard practice and could be detrimental to one or both parties. This omission could violate laws enforcing contract enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts favors severability to enforce valid portions of an agreement, when possible. By stating the entire agreement is void, this modification contradicts the principle of severability, and renders the contract unenforceable even if the invalid clause is not central to the agreement's purpose.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a severability clause that aims to maintain the contract's intent even if some parts are invalid. The changed text removes this, stating that the entire agreement becomes void if any provision is unenforceable. The Law Explanation indicates that this contradicts the principle of severability favored by the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which aims to enforce valid parts of agreements when possible. The changed text thus creates a legal contradiction by potentially nullifying the entire contract due to a minor issue, contrary to the intent to preserve agreements as much as possible. While the scraped snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation highlights a clear contradiction with legal principles that favor contract enforceability through severability.
